    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→`roko-mcp-slack` is an MCP server that exposes 8 Slack API tools via the Model Context
    14→Protocol. It enables agents to send messages, manage threads, post files, and interact with
    15→Slack channels for notifications and collaboration.
    16→
    17→**Status:** Planned (spec complete, implementation pending)
    18→
    19→**Crate:** `crates/roko-mcp-slack/`
    20→
    21→**Protocol:** MCP (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio)
    22→
    23→**Authentication:** Slack Bot Token + Signing Secret
    24→
    25→**Agent templates using this:** slack-notify-agent, pm-health-agent, action-tracker-agent,
    26→sync-agent, digest-agent
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## Connection Modes
    31→
    32→### Socket Mode (Preferred)
    33→
    34→WebSocket-based connection. No public endpoint needed. The MCP server connects to Slack's
    35→WebSocket endpoint and receives events directly.
    36→
    37→**Advantages:**
    38→- No public URL required (works behind NAT/firewall)
    39→- Real-time event delivery
    40→- Automatic reconnection
    41→- Suitable for self-hosted deployments
    42→
    43→**Configuration:**
    44→
    45→```toml
    46→[[agent.mcp_servers]]
    47→name = "slack"
    48→command = "roko-mcp-slack"
    49→args = ["--mode", "socket"]
    50→env = {
    51→    SLACK_BOT_TOKEN = "${SLACK_BOT_TOKEN}",
    52→    SLACK_APP_TOKEN = "${SLACK_APP_TOKEN}",
    53→}
    54→```
    55→
    56→### HTTP Mode (Fallback)
    57→
    58→Webhook-based connection. Requires a publicly accessible URL for Slack to POST events to.
    59→
    60→**Configuration:**
    61→
    62→```toml
    63→[[agent.mcp_servers]]
    64→name = "slack"
    65→command = "roko-mcp-slack"
    66→args = ["--mode", "http", "--port", "3001"]
    67→env = {
    68→    SLACK_BOT_TOKEN = "${SLACK_BOT_TOKEN}",
    69→    SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET = "${SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET}",
    70→}
    71→```
    72→
    73→---
    74→
    75→## The 8 Tools
    76→
    77→### 1. `slack.post_message`
    78→
    79→Post a message to a Slack channel.
    80→
    81→```json
    82→{
    83→  "name": "post_message",
    84→  "description": "Post a message to a Slack channel. Supports Block Kit formatting.",
    85→  "inputSchema": {
    86→    "type": "object",
    87→    "properties": {
    88→      "channel": {
    89→        "type": "string",
    90→        "description": "Channel name (e.g., '#roko-activity') or channel ID"
    91→      },
    92→      "text": {
    93→        "type": "string",
    94→        "description": "Message text (used as fallback for Block Kit)"
    95→      },
    96→      "blocks": {
    97→        "type": "array",
    98→        "description": "Block Kit blocks for rich formatting (optional)",
    99→        "items": { "type": "object" }
   100→      },
   101→      "thread_ts": {
   102→        "type": "string",
   103→        "description": "Thread timestamp to reply in a thread (optional)"
   104→      }
   105→    },
   106→    "required": ["channel", "text"]
   107→  }
   108→}
   109→```
   110→
   111→### 2. `slack.update_message`
   112→
   113→Update an existing message.
   114→
   115→```json
   116→{
   117→  "name": "update_message",
   118→  "description": "Update a previously sent message. Requires the message timestamp.",
   119→  "inputSchema": {
   120→    "type": "object",
   121→    "properties": {
   122→      "channel": { "type": "string" },
   123→      "ts": { "type": "string", "description": "Message timestamp (from post_message response)" },
   124→      "text": { "type": "string" },
   125→      "blocks": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "object" } }
   126→    },
   127→    "required": ["channel", "ts", "text"]
   128→  }
   129→}
   130→```
   131→
   132→### 3. `slack.reply_thread`
   133→
   134→Reply in a specific thread.
   135→
   136→```json
   137→{
   138→  "name": "reply_thread",
   139→  "description": "Reply in a thread. Use the parent message's ts as thread_ts.",
   140→  "inputSchema": {
   141→    "type": "object",
   142→    "properties": {
   143→      "channel": { "type": "string" },
   144→      "thread_ts": { "type": "string", "description": "Parent message timestamp" },
   145→      "text": { "type": "string" },
   146→      "blocks": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "object" } }
   147→    },
   148→    "required": ["channel", "thread_ts", "text"]
   149→  }
   150→}
   151→```
   152→
   153→### 4. `slack.add_reaction`
   154→
   155→Add an emoji reaction to a message.
   156→
   157→```json
   158→{
   159→  "name": "add_reaction",
   160→  "description": "Add an emoji reaction to a message.",
   161→  "inputSchema": {
   162→    "type": "object",
   163→    "properties": {
   164→      "channel": { "type": "string" },
   165→      "ts": { "type": "string", "description": "Message timestamp" },
   166→      "emoji": { "type": "string", "description": "Emoji name without colons (e.g., 'thumbsup')" }
   167→    },
   168→    "required": ["channel", "ts", "emoji"]
   169→  }
   170→}
   171→```
   172→
   173→### 5. `slack.upload_file`
   174→
   175→Upload a file to a channel.
   176→
   177→```json
   178→{
   179→  "name": "upload_file",
   180→  "description": "Upload a file to a Slack channel.",
   181→  "inputSchema": {
   182→    "type": "object",
   183→    "properties": {
   184→      "channel": { "type": "string" },
   185→      "content": { "type": "string", "description": "File content (text)" },
   186→      "filename": { "type": "string" },
   187→      "filetype": { "type": "string", "description": "File type (e.g., 'markdown', 'json', 'text')" },
   188→      "title": { "type": "string" },
   189→      "initial_comment": { "type": "string" }
   190→    },
   191→    "required": ["channel", "content", "filename"]
   192→  }
   193→}
   194→```
   195→
   196→### 6. `slack.get_channel_history`
   197→
   198→Get recent messages from a channel.
   199→
   200→```json
   201→{
   202→  "name": "get_channel_history",
   203→  "description": "Get recent messages from a channel. Returns up to 'limit' messages.",
   204→  "inputSchema": {
   205→    "type": "object",
   206→    "properties": {
   207→      "channel": { "type": "string" },
   208→      "limit": { "type": "integer", "default": 20, "maximum": 100 },
   209→      "oldest": { "type": "string", "description": "Unix timestamp — only messages after this time" }
   210→    },
   211→    "required": ["channel"]
   212→  }
   213→}
   214→```
   215→
   216→### 7. `slack.get_thread`
   217→
   218→Get all replies in a thread.
   219→
   220→```json
   221→{
   222→  "name": "get_thread",
   223→  "description": "Get all replies in a thread.",
   224→  "inputSchema": {
   225→    "type": "object",
   226→    "properties": {
   227→      "channel": { "type": "string" },
   228→      "thread_ts": { "type": "string", "description": "Parent message timestamp" },
   229→      "limit": { "type": "integer", "default": 50 }
   230→    },
   231→    "required": ["channel", "thread_ts"]
   232→  }
   233→}
   234→```
   235→
   236→### 8. `slack.lookup_user`
   237→
   238→Look up a Slack user by email or display name.
   239→
   240→```json
   241→{
   242→  "name": "lookup_user",
   243→  "description": "Look up a Slack user by email or display name.",
   244→  "inputSchema": {
   245→    "type": "object",
   246→    "properties": {
   247→      "email": { "type": "string", "description": "User's email address" },
   248→      "name": { "type": "string", "description": "User's display name (fuzzy match)" }
   249→    }
   250→  }
   251→}
   252→```
   253→
   254→---
   255→
   256→## Rate Limiting
   257→
   258→Slack API rate limits are handled transparently by the MCP server:
   259→
   260→| API Method | Rate Limit | Tier |
   261→|---|---|---|
   262→| `chat.postMessage` | 1 per second per channel | Tier 3 |
   263→| `chat.update` | 1 per second per channel | Tier 3 |
   264→| `files.upload` | 20 per minute | Tier 2 |
   265→| `conversations.history` | 50 per minute | Tier 3 |
   266→| `conversations.replies` | 50 per minute | Tier 3 |
   267→| `users.lookupByEmail` | 50 per minute | Tier 3 |
   268→| `reactions.add` | 50 per minute | Tier 3 |
   269→
   270→The server implements a token bucket rate limiter per endpoint. When rate-limited, it queues
   271→the request and retries with exponential backoff.
   272→
   273→---
   274→
   275→## Slack Bot Permissions (Scopes)
   276→
   277→Required bot token scopes:
   278→
   279→| Scope | Used By |
   280→|---|---|
   281→| `chat:write` | post_message, update_message, reply_thread |
   282→| `files:write` | upload_file |
   283→| `reactions:write` | add_reaction |
   284→| `channels:history` | get_channel_history, get_thread |
   285→| `channels:read` | Channel ID resolution |
   286→| `users:read` | lookup_user |
   287→| `users:read.email` | lookup_user (by email) |
   288→
   289→---
   290→
   291→## Block Kit Integration
   292→
   293→The `post_message`, `update_message`, and `reply_thread` tools support Slack's Block Kit for
   294→rich message formatting. When `blocks` is provided, `text` serves as the fallback for
   295→notifications and accessibility.
   296→
   297→Example Block Kit usage by the slack-notify-agent:
   298→
   299→```json
   300→{
   301→  "channel": "#roko-alerts",
   302→  "text": "Gate failure for code-implementer-agent",
   303→  "blocks": [
   304→    {
   305→      "type": "header",
   306→      "text": { "type": "plain_text", "text": "Gate Failure" }
   307→    },
   308→    {
   309→      "type": "section",
   310→      "fields": [
   311→        { "type": "mrkdwn", "text": "*Agent:* code-implementer-agent" },
   312→        { "type": "mrkdwn", "text": "*Gate:* CompileGate" },
   313→        { "type": "mrkdwn", "text": "*Error:* `error[E0308]: mismatched types`" }
   314→      ]
   315→    },
   316→    {
   317→      "type": "actions",
   318→      "elements": [
   319→        {
   320→          "type": "button",
   321→          "text": { "type": "plain_text", "text": "View PR" },
   322→          "url": "https://github.com/nunchi/roko/pull/42"
   323→        }
   324→      ]
   325→    }
   326→  ]
   327→}
   328→```
   329→
   330→---
   331→
   332→## Agents & Assistants API Integration
   333→
   334→Slack's Agents & Assistants API (available since 2024) provides a framework for building
   335→conversational agents within Slack. Future versions of `roko-mcp-slack` may support this API
   336→for interactive agent sessions directly in Slack threads — enabling users to converse with
   337→Roko agents without leaving Slack.
   338→
   339→Current scope: one-way notifications and channel operations. Interactive sessions are a future
   340→enhancement.
   341→

1→# 12 — roko-mcp-scripts
     2→
     3→> Config-driven tool wrappers: scripts.toml format, executor, discovery,
     4→> collaboration scripts, knowledge-base scripts.
     5→
     6→
     7→> **Implementation**: Scaffold
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→`roko-mcp-scripts` is an MCP server that wraps arbitrary shell scripts and executables as
    14→MCP tools. It provides **zero-code tool extension** — add a TOML configuration entry and a
    15→script, and it becomes an agent-callable tool with JSON Schema validation, structured output
    16→parsing, and timeout handling.
    17→
    18→**Status:** Planned (spec complete, implementation pending)
    19→
    20→**Crate:** `crates/roko-mcp-scripts/`
    21→
    22→**Protocol:** MCP (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio)
    23→
    24→**Agent templates using this:** pm-board-agent, enrich-agent, triage-agent, pm-health-agent,
    25→action-tracker-agent, sync-agent, freshness-agent, digest-agent, conflict-detector-agent
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## Architecture
    30→
    31→```
    32→roko-mcp-scripts
    33→    ├── config.rs        # Parse scripts.toml
    34→    ├── executor.rs      # Run scripts, capture output, enforce timeouts
    35→    ├── discovery.rs     # Enumerate available scripts from config
    36→    └── main.rs          # MCP server (stdio transport)
    37→```
    38→
    39→### How It Works
    40→
    41→1. On startup, `roko-mcp-scripts` reads a `scripts.toml` configuration file
    42→2. Each entry in the config becomes an MCP tool with:
    43→   - A name (derived from the TOML key)
    44→   - A description (from the TOML `description` field)
    45→   - An input schema (from the TOML `params` field, mapped to JSON Schema)
    46→   - A handler that invokes the configured script/command
    47→3. Tool discovery (`tools/list`) returns all configured scripts
    48→4. Tool execution (`tools/call`) runs the script with parameters as environment variables
    49→   or command-line arguments
    50→
    51→### Executor
    52→
    53→```rust
    54→pub struct ScriptExecutor {
    55→    /// Working directory for script execution.
    56→    working_dir: PathBuf,
    57→    /// Default timeout for script execution.
    58→    default_timeout: Duration,
    59→    /// Environment variables passed to all scripts.
    60→    base_env: HashMap<String, String>,
    61→}
    62→
    63→impl ScriptExecutor {
    64→    pub async fn execute(
    65→        &self,
    66→        script: &ScriptConfig,
    67→        params: &serde_json::Value,
    68→    ) -> Result<serde_json::Value> {
    69→        let mut cmd = tokio::process::Command::new(&script.command);
    70→        cmd.current_dir(&self.working_dir);
    71→
    72→        // Pass parameters as environment variables
    73→        for (key, value) in params.as_object().unwrap_or(&serde_json::Map::new()) {
    74→            cmd.env(format!("PARAM_{}", key.to_uppercase()), value.to_string());
    75→        }
    76→
    77→        // Or as command-line arguments (based on script config)
    78→        if script.pass_as == "args" {
    79→            for arg in &script.args {
    80→                let resolved = resolve_param(arg, params)?;
    81→                cmd.arg(resolved);
    82→            }
    83→        }
    84→
    85→        // Set timeout
    86→        let timeout = script.timeout.unwrap_or(self.default_timeout);
    87→        let output = tokio::time::timeout(timeout, cmd.output()).await??;
    88→
    89→        // Parse output
    90→        if script.output_format == "json" {
    91→            Ok(serde_json::from_slice(&output.stdout)?)
    92→        } else {
    93→            Ok(json!({ "stdout": String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout) }))
    94→        }
    95→    }
    96→}
    97→```
    98→
    99→---
   100→
   101→## scripts.toml Format
   102→
   103→```toml
   104→# scripts.toml — Tool definitions for roko-mcp-scripts
   105→
   106→[meta]
   107→working_dir = "/path/to/repo"
   108→default_timeout = "30s"
   109→
   110→# Each [[tool]] entry becomes an MCP tool
   111→[[tool]]
   112→name = "pm_sync"
   113→description = "Synchronize PM state between GitHub and TOML task files"
   114→command = "node"
   115→args = ["scripts/pm/sync.js", "--direction", "${direction}"]
   116→timeout = "60s"
   117→output_format = "json"
   118→
   119→[tool.params]
   120→direction = { type = "string", enum = ["push", "pull"], description = "Sync direction" }
   121→
   122→[[tool]]
   123→name = "pm_validate"
   124→description = "Validate referential integrity of PM TOML task files"
   125→command = "node"
   126→args = ["scripts/pm/validate.js"]
   127→output_format = "json"
   128→
   129→[[tool]]
   130→name = "pm_views"
   131→description = "Regenerate PM dashboard views (board, health, people, timeline)"
   132→command = "node"
   133→args = ["scripts/pm/views.js", "--view", "${view}"]
   134→
   135→[tool.params]
   136→view = { type = "string", enum = ["all", "board", "health", "people", "timeline"], default = "all" }
   137→```
   138→
   139→### Config Schema
   140→
   141→| Field | Type | Required | Description |
   142→|---|---|---|---|
   143→| `name` | string | Yes | Tool name (becomes `scripts.<name>` in MCP) |
   144→| `description` | string | Yes | LLM-facing description |
   145→| `command` | string | Yes | Executable to run |
   146→| `args` | array of string | No | Command-line arguments. `${param}` syntax resolves from input. |
   147→| `timeout` | duration string | No | Max execution time (default from `[meta]`) |
   148→| `output_format` | string | No | `"json"` or `"text"` (default: `"text"`) |
   149→| `env` | table | No | Additional environment variables |
   150→| `params` | table | No | Input parameters (mapped to JSON Schema) |
   151→| `pass_as` | string | No | `"env"` (default) or `"args"` — how params are passed to script |
   152→
   153→---
   154→
   155→## Collaboration Repo Scripts
   156→
   157→Scripts for the collaboration repository (`/Users/will/dev/nunchi/collaboration/`):
   158→
   159→```toml
   160→# collaboration/.roko/scripts.toml
   161→
   162→[[tool]]
   163→name = "doc_lifecycle_check"
   164→description = "Check document lifecycle status (draft/review/canonical/archived)"
   165→command = "node"
   166→args = ["scripts/doc-lifecycle/check.js", "--path", "${path}"]
   167→output_format = "json"
   168→
   169→[tool.params]
   170→path = { type = "string", description = "Path to document" }
   171→
   172→[[tool]]
   173→name = "generate_digest"
   174→description = "Generate weekly digest of document changes across repos"
   175→command = "node"
   176→args = ["scripts/digest/generate.js", "--repos", "${repos}", "--since", "${since}"]
   177→output_format = "json"
   178→
   179→[tool.params]
   180→repos = { type = "string", default = "collaboration,knowledge-base,roko" }
   181→since = { type = "string", default = "7d", description = "Time period (e.g., '7d', '2w')" }
   182→
   183→[[tool]]
   184→name = "sync_repos"
   185→description = "Synchronize documents between collaboration and knowledge-base repos"
   186→command = "node"
   187→args = ["scripts/sync/run.js"]
   188→output_format = "json"
   189→
   190→[[tool]]
   191→name = "detect_conflicts"
   192→description = "Detect conflicting claims across documents"
   193→command = "node"
   194→args = ["scripts/conflicts/detect.js", "--path", "${path}"]
   195→output_format = "json"
   196→
   197→[tool.params]
   198→path = { type = "string", default = "docs/", description = "Directory to scan" }
   199→
   200→[[tool]]
   201→name = "check_freshness"
   202→description = "Check document freshness and flag stale content"
   203→command = "node"
   204→args = ["scripts/freshness/check.js", "--max-age", "${max_age}"]
   205→output_format = "json"
   206→
   207→[tool.params]
   208→max_age = { type = "string", default = "30d", description = "Maximum age before flagging" }
   209→
   210→[[tool]]
   211→name = "process_meeting"
   212→description = "Extract action items and decisions from meeting notes"
   213→command = "node"
   214→args = ["scripts/meetings/process.js", "--path", "${path}"]
   215→output_format = "json"
   216→
   217→[tool.params]
   218→path = { type = "string", description = "Path to meeting notes file" }
   219→```
   220→
   221→---
   222→
   223→## Knowledge-Base Repo Scripts
   224→
   225→Scripts for the knowledge-base repository:
   226→
   227→```toml
   228→# knowledge-base/.roko/scripts.toml
   229→
   230→[[tool]]
   231→name = "pm_sync"
   232→description = "Synchronize PM state between GitHub and TOML task files"
   233→command = "node"
   234→args = ["scripts/pm/sync.js", "--direction", "${direction}"]
   235→output_format = "json"
   236→
   237→[tool.params]
   238→direction = { type = "string", enum = ["push", "pull"] }
   239→
   240→[[tool]]
   241→name = "pm_validate"
   242→description = "Validate referential integrity of PM TOML task files"
   243→command = "node"
   244→args = ["scripts/pm/validate.js"]
   245→output_format = "json"
   246→
   247→[[tool]]
   248→name = "pm_views"
   249→description = "Regenerate PM dashboard views"
   250→command = "node"
   251→args = ["scripts/pm/views.js", "--view", "${view}"]
   252→output_format = "json"
   253→
   254→[tool.params]
   255→view = { type = "string", enum = ["all", "board", "health", "people", "timeline"], default = "all" }
   256→
   257→[[tool]]
   258→name = "extract_actions"
   259→description = "Extract action items from recent documents"
   260→command = "node"
   261→args = ["scripts/actions/extract.js", "--since", "${since}"]
   262→output_format = "json"
   263→
   264→[tool.params]
   265→since = { type = "string", default = "24h" }
   266→
   267→[[tool]]
   268→name = "deep_enrich"
   269→description = "Enrich a document with cross-references, citations, and related links"
   270→command = "node"
   271→args = ["scripts/enrich/deep.js", "--path", "${path}", "--depth", "${depth}"]
   272→output_format = "json"
   273→
   274→[tool.params]
   275→path = { type = "string", description = "Path to document" }
   276→depth = { type = "string", enum = ["light", "deep"], default = "deep" }
   277→```
   278→
   279→---
   280→
   281→## Security Considerations
   282→
   283→### Script Execution Isolation
   284→
   285→Scripts run as child processes with:
   286→- **Working directory** constrained to the configured repo root
   287→- **Timeout enforcement** via `tokio::time::timeout`
   288→- **Environment isolation** — only explicitly declared env vars plus PARAM_* vars
   289→- **No stdin** — scripts receive input via env vars or args, not stdin (preventing injection)
   290→
   291→### Input Validation
   292→
   293→Parameters are validated against the JSON Schema derived from `[tool.params]` before the
   294→script is invoked. Invalid parameters are rejected with a structured error — the script
   295→never sees them.
   296→
   297→### Output Sanitization
   298→
   299→Script output is parsed and sanitized:
   300→- JSON output: validated as valid JSON
   301→- Text output: wrapped in `{ "stdout": "..." }`
   302→- stderr: captured but not returned to the LLM (logged for debugging)
   303→- Exit codes: non-zero exit → error result with stderr content
   304→
   305→---
   306→
   307→## Discovery Mechanism
   308→
   309→On `tools/list`, `roko-mcp-scripts` reads the configured `scripts.toml` and returns all
   310→`[[tool]]` entries as MCP tools:
   311→
   312→```rust
   313→pub fn discover_tools(config: &ScriptsConfig) -> Vec<McpTool> {
   314→    config.tools.iter().map(|tool| McpTool {
   315→        name: tool.name.clone(),
   316→        description: tool.description.clone(),
   317→        input_schema: generate_schema(&tool.params),
   318→    }).collect()
   319→}
   320→```
   321→
   322→This means agents automatically see new tools when entries are added to `scripts.toml` — no
   323→recompilation needed. The agent just needs to be restarted (or the MCP server reconnected) to
   324→pick up new tools.
   325→

# 12 — roko-mcp-scripts

> Config-driven tool wrappers: scripts.toml format, executor, discovery,
> collaboration scripts, knowledge-base scripts.


> **Implementation**: Scaffold

---

## Overview

`roko-mcp-scripts` is an MCP server that wraps arbitrary shell scripts and executables as
MCP tools. It provides **zero-code tool extension** — add a TOML configuration entry and a
script, and it becomes an agent-callable tool with JSON Schema validation, structured output
parsing, and timeout handling.

**Status:** Planned (spec complete, implementation pending)

**Crate:** `crates/roko-mcp-scripts/`

**Protocol:** MCP (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio)

**Agent templates using this:** pm-board-agent, enrich-agent, triage-agent, pm-health-agent,
action-tracker-agent, sync-agent, freshness-agent, digest-agent, conflict-detector-agent

---

## Architecture

```
roko-mcp-scripts
    ├── config.rs        # Parse scripts.toml
    ├── executor.rs      # Run scripts, capture output, enforce timeouts
    ├── discovery.rs     # Enumerate available scripts from config
    └── main.rs          # MCP server (stdio transport)
```

### How It Works

1. On startup, `roko-mcp-scripts` reads a `scripts.toml` configuration file
2. Each entry in the config becomes an MCP tool with:
   - A name (derived from the TOML key)
   - A description (from the TOML `description` field)
   - An input schema (from the TOML `params` field, mapped to JSON Schema)
   - A handler that invokes the configured script/command
3. Tool discovery (`tools/list`) returns all configured scripts
4. Tool execution (`tools/call`) runs the script with parameters as environment variables
   or command-line arguments

### Executor

```rust
pub struct ScriptExecutor {
    /// Working directory for script execution.
    working_dir: PathBuf,
    /// Default timeout for script execution.
    default_timeout: Duration,
    /// Environment variables passed to all scripts.
    base_env: HashMap<String, String>,
}

impl ScriptExecutor {
    pub async fn execute(
        &self,
        script: &ScriptConfig,
        params: &serde_json::Value,
    ) -> Result<serde_json::Value> {
        let mut cmd = tokio::process::Command::new(&script.command);
        cmd.current_dir(&self.working_dir);

        // Pass parameters as environment variables
        for (key, value) in params.as_object().unwrap_or(&serde_json::Map::new()) {
            cmd.env(format!("PARAM_{}", key.to_uppercase()), value.to_string());
        }

        // Or as command-line arguments (based on script config)
        if script.pass_as == "args" {
            for arg in &script.args {
                let resolved = resolve_param(arg, params)?;
                cmd.arg(resolved);
            }
        }

        // Set timeout
        let timeout = script.timeout.unwrap_or(self.default_timeout);
        let output = tokio::time::timeout(timeout, cmd.output()).await??;

        // Parse output
        if script.output_format == "json" {
            Ok(serde_json::from_slice(&output.stdout)?)
        } else {
            Ok(json!({ "stdout": String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout) }))
        }
    }
}
```

---

## scripts.toml Format

```toml
# scripts.toml — Tool definitions for roko-mcp-scripts

[meta]
working_dir = "/path/to/repo"
default_timeout = "30s"

# Each [[tool]] entry becomes an MCP tool
[[tool]]
name = "pm_sync"
description = "Synchronize PM state between GitHub and TOML task files"
command = "node"
args = ["scripts/pm/sync.js", "--direction", "${direction}"]
timeout = "60s"
output_format = "json"

[tool.params]
direction = { type = "string", enum = ["push", "pull"], description = "Sync direction" }

[[tool]]
name = "pm_validate"
description = "Validate referential integrity of PM TOML task files"
command = "node"
args = ["scripts/pm/validate.js"]
output_format = "json"

[[tool]]
name = "pm_views"
description = "Regenerate PM dashboard views (board, health, people, timeline)"
command = "node"
args = ["scripts/pm/views.js", "--view", "${view}"]

[tool.params]
view = { type = "string", enum = ["all", "board", "health", "people", "timeline"], default = "all" }
```

### Config Schema

| Field | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `name` | string | Yes | Tool name (becomes `scripts.<name>` in MCP) |
| `description` | string | Yes | LLM-facing description |
| `command` | string | Yes | Executable to run |
| `args` | array of string | No | Command-line arguments. `${param}` syntax resolves from input. |
| `timeout` | duration string | No | Max execution time (default from `[meta]`) |
| `output_format` | string | No | `"json"` or `"text"` (default: `"text"`) |
| `env` | table | No | Additional environment variables |
| `params` | table | No | Input parameters (mapped to JSON Schema) |
| `pass_as` | string | No | `"env"` (default) or `"args"` — how params are passed to script |

## Security Considerations

### Script Execution Isolation

Scripts run as child processes with:
- **Working directory** constrained to the configured repo root
- **Timeout enforcement** via `tokio::time::timeout`
- **Environment isolation** — only explicitly declared env vars plus PARAM_* vars
- **No stdin** — scripts receive input via env vars or args, not stdin (preventing injection)

### Input Validation

Parameters are validated against the JSON Schema derived from `[tool.params]` before the
script is invoked. Invalid parameters are rejected with a structured error — the script
never sees them.

### Output Sanitization

Script output is parsed and sanitized:
- JSON output: validated as valid JSON
- Text output: wrapped in `{ "stdout": "..." }`
- stderr: captured but not returned to the LLM (logged for debugging)
- Exit codes: non-zero exit → error result with stderr content

---

## Discovery Mechanism

On `tools/list`, `roko-mcp-scripts` reads the configured `scripts.toml` and returns all
`[[tool]]` entries as MCP tools:

```rust
pub fn discover_tools(config: &ScriptsConfig) -> Vec<McpTool> {
    config.tools.iter().map(|tool| McpTool {
        name: tool.name.clone(),
        description: tool.description.clone(),
        input_schema: generate_schema(&tool.params),
    }).collect()
}
```

This means agents automatically see new tools when entries are added to `scripts.toml` — no
recompilation needed. The agent just needs to be restarted (or the MCP server reconnected) to
pick up new tools.

1→# 13 — roko-mcp-stdio
     2→
     3→> Generic MCP stdio scaffold: protocol handler, tool registration,
     4→> base implementation for building custom MCP servers.
     5→
     6→
     7→> **Implementation**: Scaffold
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→`roko-mcp-stdio` is a scaffold crate that provides the base implementation for building
    14→MCP servers in Rust. It handles the MCP protocol (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio), tool registration,
    15→request routing, and error handling — so that custom MCP servers only need to implement their
    16→tool-specific logic.
    17→
    18→**Status:** Scaffold (base implementation, not feature-complete)
    19→
    20→**Purpose:** Shared foundation for `roko-mcp-github`, `roko-mcp-slack`, `roko-mcp-scripts`,
    21→and any custom MCP servers.
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## Architecture
    26→
    27→```
    28→roko-mcp-stdio
    29→    ├── protocol.rs     # JSON-RPC 2.0 message types and parsing
    30→    ├── server.rs       # Main event loop (read stdin, dispatch, write stdout)
    31→    ├── registry.rs     # Tool registration and schema generation
    32→    ├── handler.rs      # Trait for tool implementations
    33→    └── lib.rs          # Public API
    34→```
    35→
    36→### Core Trait: McpToolHandler
    37→
    38→Custom MCP servers implement this trait for each tool:
    39→
    40→```rust
    41→#[async_trait]
    42→pub trait McpToolHandler: Send + Sync {
    43→    /// Tool name (returned in tools/list).
    44→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    45→
    46→    /// Tool description (LLM-facing).
    47→    fn description(&self) -> &str;
    48→
    49→    /// JSON Schema for input parameters.
    50→    fn input_schema(&self) -> serde_json::Value;
    51→
    52→    /// Execute the tool with the given arguments.
    53→    async fn execute(
    54→        &self,
    55→        arguments: serde_json::Value,
    56→    ) -> Result<McpToolResult>;
    57→}
    58→
    59→pub struct McpToolResult {
    60→    pub content: Vec<McpContent>,
    61→    pub is_error: bool,
    62→}
    63→
    64→pub enum McpContent {
    65→    Text { text: String },
    66→    Image { data: String, mime_type: String },
    67→    Resource { uri: String, text: String },
    68→}
    69→```
    70→
    71→### Server Builder
    72→
    73→```rust
    74→pub struct McpServerBuilder {
    75→    tools: Vec<Box<dyn McpToolHandler>>,
    76→    name: String,
    77→    version: String,
    78→}
    79→
    80→impl McpServerBuilder {
    81→    pub fn new(name: &str, version: &str) -> Self {
    82→        Self {
    83→            tools: Vec::new(),
    84→            name: name.to_string(),
    85→            version: version.to_string(),
    86→        }
    87→    }
    88→
    89→    /// Register a tool handler.
    90→    pub fn tool(mut self, handler: impl McpToolHandler + 'static) -> Self {
    91→        self.tools.push(Box::new(handler));
    92→        self
    93→    }
    94→
    95→    /// Start the MCP server (reads from stdin, writes to stdout).
    96→    pub async fn serve(self) -> Result<()> {
    97→        let server = McpServer::new(self.name, self.version, self.tools);
    98→        server.run().await
    99→    }
   100→}
   101→```
   102→
   103→### Usage Example
   104→
   105→Building a custom MCP server using `roko-mcp-stdio`:
   106→
   107→```rust
   108→use roko_mcp_stdio::{McpServerBuilder, McpToolHandler, McpToolResult, McpContent};
   109→
   110→struct MyTool;
   111→
   112→#[async_trait]
   113→impl McpToolHandler for MyTool {
   114→    fn name(&self) -> &str { "my_tool" }
   115→    fn description(&self) -> &str { "Does something useful" }
   116→    fn input_schema(&self) -> serde_json::Value {
   117→        json!({
   118→            "type": "object",
   119→            "properties": {
   120→                "input": { "type": "string" }
   121→            },
   122→            "required": ["input"]
   123→        })
   124→    }
   125→
   126→    async fn execute(&self, arguments: serde_json::Value) -> Result<McpToolResult> {
   127→        let input = arguments["input"].as_str().unwrap_or("");
   128→        Ok(McpToolResult {
   129→            content: vec![McpContent::Text {
   130→                text: format!("Processed: {input}"),
   131→            }],
   132→            is_error: false,
   133→        })
   134→    }
   135→}
   136→
   137→#[tokio::main]
   138→async fn main() -> Result<()> {
   139→    McpServerBuilder::new("my-mcp-server", "0.1.0")
   140→        .tool(MyTool)
   141→        .serve()
   142→        .await
   143→}
   144→```
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## Protocol Implementation
   149→
   150→### JSON-RPC 2.0 Messages
   151→
   152→```rust
   153→#[derive(Deserialize)]
   154→pub struct JsonRpcRequest {
   155→    pub jsonrpc: String,    // Must be "2.0"
   156→    pub method: String,
   157→    pub params: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   158→    pub id: serde_json::Value,
   159→}
   160→
   161→#[derive(Serialize)]
   162→pub struct JsonRpcResponse {
   163→    pub jsonrpc: String,    // "2.0"
   164→    pub result: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   165→    pub error: Option<JsonRpcError>,
   166→    pub id: serde_json::Value,
   167→}
   168→
   169→#[derive(Serialize)]
   170→pub struct JsonRpcError {
   171→    pub code: i32,
   172→    pub message: String,
   173→    pub data: Option<serde_json::Value>,
   174→}
   175→```
   176→
   177→### Supported Methods
   178→
   179→| Method | Description |
   180→|---|---|
   181→| `initialize` | Server handshake (capabilities exchange) |
   182→| `tools/list` | List all registered tools with schemas |
   183→| `tools/call` | Execute a tool with arguments |
   184→| `notifications/initialized` | Client confirmation (no response) |
   185→
   186→### Server Event Loop
   187→
   188→```rust
   189→pub struct McpServer {
   190→    name: String,
   191→    version: String,
   192→    tools: HashMap<String, Box<dyn McpToolHandler>>,
   193→}
   194→
   195→impl McpServer {
   196→    pub async fn run(&self) -> Result<()> {
   197→        let stdin = tokio::io::stdin();
   198→        let stdout = tokio::io::stdout();
   199→        let mut reader = BufReader::new(stdin);
   200→        let mut writer = BufWriter::new(stdout);
   201→
   202→        loop {
   203→            let mut line = String::new();
   204→            let bytes_read = reader.read_line(&mut line).await?;
   205→            if bytes_read == 0 { break; } // EOF
   206→
   207→            let request: JsonRpcRequest = serde_json::from_str(&line)?;
   208→            let response = self.dispatch(&request).await;
   209→            let response_json = serde_json::to_string(&response)?;
   210→
   211→            writer.write_all(response_json.as_bytes()).await?;
   212→            writer.write_all(b"\n").await?;
   213→            writer.flush().await?;
   214→        }
   215→
   216→        Ok(())
   217→    }
   218→
   219→    async fn dispatch(&self, request: &JsonRpcRequest) -> JsonRpcResponse {
   220→        match request.method.as_str() {
   221→            "initialize" => self.handle_initialize(request),
   222→            "tools/list" => self.handle_tools_list(request),
   223→            "tools/call" => self.handle_tools_call(request).await,
   224→            "notifications/initialized" => return, // No response for notifications
   225→            _ => self.method_not_found(request),
   226→        }
   227→    }
   228→}
   229→```
   230→
   231→---
   232→
   233→## Tool Registry
   234→
   235→```rust
   236→pub struct ToolRegistry {
   237→    tools: Vec<Box<dyn McpToolHandler>>,
   238→}
   239→
   240→impl ToolRegistry {
   241→    /// Generate the tools/list response.
   242→    pub fn list(&self) -> serde_json::Value {
   243→        json!({
   244→            "tools": self.tools.iter().map(|t| json!({
   245→                "name": t.name(),
   246→                "description": t.description(),
   247→                "inputSchema": t.input_schema(),
   248→            })).collect::<Vec<_>>()
   249→        })
   250→    }
   251→
   252→    /// Find a tool by name.
   253→    pub fn get(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&dyn McpToolHandler> {
   254→        self.tools.iter().find(|t| t.name() == name).map(|t| t.as_ref())
   255→    }
   256→}
   257→```
   258→
   259→---
   260→
   261→## Error Handling
   262→
   263→Standard JSON-RPC 2.0 error codes:
   264→
   265→| Code | Meaning | When |
   266→|---|---|---|
   267→| -32700 | Parse error | Malformed JSON |
   268→| -32600 | Invalid request | Missing jsonrpc/method |
   269→| -32601 | Method not found | Unknown method |
   270→| -32602 | Invalid params | Parameters don't match schema |
   271→| -32603 | Internal error | Tool execution failure |
   272→
   273→---
   274→
   275→## Extension Points
   276→
   277→### Custom Transports
   278→
   279→While `roko-mcp-stdio` defaults to stdio, the protocol layer is transport-agnostic. Custom
   280→transports (WebSocket, HTTP SSE) can be implemented by providing alternative read/write
   281→implementations.
   282→
   283→### Middleware
   284→
   285→The server supports middleware for cross-cutting concerns:
   286→
   287→```rust
   288→pub trait McpMiddleware: Send + Sync {
   289→    async fn before_tool_call(
   290→        &self,
   291→        tool_name: &str,
   292→        arguments: &serde_json::Value,
   293→    ) -> Result<()>;
   294→
   295→    async fn after_tool_call(
   296→        &self,
   297→        tool_name: &str,
   298→        result: &McpToolResult,
   299→        duration: Duration,
   300→    );
   301→}
   302→```
   303→
   304→Use cases for middleware:
   305→- **Logging**: Record all tool calls for debugging
   306→- **Metrics**: Track tool execution latency and error rates
   307→- **Rate limiting**: Enforce per-tool rate limits
   308→- **Caching**: Cache deterministic tool results
   309→
   310→---
   311→
   312→## Relationship to Other MCP Crates
   313→
   314→```
   315→roko-mcp-stdio (this crate)
   316→    ↑ depends on
   317→    ├── roko-mcp-github  (17 tools)
   318→    ├── roko-mcp-slack   (8 tools)
   319→    └── roko-mcp-scripts (N tools, config-driven)
   320→```
   321→
   322→Each MCP server crate depends on `roko-mcp-stdio` for the protocol handling and implements
   323→`McpToolHandler` for its specific tools. This ensures consistent protocol behavior across all
   324→MCP servers.
   325→

1→# 14 — roko-plugin SDK
     2→
     3→> EventSource, FeedbackCollector, Integration traits. How to build domain plugins
     4→> and extend Roko with new capabilities.
     5→
     6→
     7→> **Implementation**: Specified
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→The `roko-plugin` crate (planned) provides the SDK for extending Roko with new event sources,
    14→feedback collectors, integrations, and domain plugins. It defines the traits that third-party
    15→code implements to participate in the Roko runtime.
    16→
    17→**Status:** Planned (trait definitions spec'd, crate not yet created)
    18→
    19→**Target crate:** `crates/roko-plugin/`
    20→
    21→**Design principle:** Plugins compose with the Synapse Architecture. A plugin implements one or
    22→more traits, registers with the runtime, and participates in the cognitive loop without
    23→modifying core code.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## Plugin Traits
    28→
    29→### EventSource
    30→
    31→Event sources produce events that trigger agent execution. They convert external signals
    32→(cron ticks, file changes, webhooks, Slack messages, chain events) into Engrams that the
    33→dispatch loop can route to agent templates.
    34→
    35→```rust
    36→#[async_trait]
    37→pub trait EventSource: Send + Sync {
    38→    /// Human-readable name for this event source.
    39→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    40→
    41→    /// Start the event source. Emits events via the provided sender.
    42→    async fn start(&self, sender: EventSender) -> Result<()>;
    43→
    44→    /// Stop the event source gracefully.
    45→    async fn stop(&self) -> Result<()>;
    46→
    47→    /// Health check — is this event source still running?
    48→    fn is_healthy(&self) -> bool;
    49→}
    50→
    51→/// Channel for emitting events from an event source.
    52→pub struct EventSender {
    53→    inner: tokio::sync::mpsc::Sender<SourceEvent>,
    54→}
    55→
    56→pub struct SourceEvent {
    57→    /// Event kind (e.g., "scheduler.cron", "watcher.fs_change", "webhook.github.push").
    58→    pub kind: String,
    59→    /// Event payload as an Engram-compatible body.
    60→    pub body: serde_json::Value,
    61→    /// Source identifier (for routing to the correct subscription).
    62→    pub source: String,
    63→}
    64→```
    65→
    66→### FeedbackCollector
    67→
    68→Feedback collectors gather outcome data from agent executions and feed it back into the
    69→learning system. They bridge the VERIFY step of the cognitive loop with external outcome
    70→sources.
    71→
    72→```rust
    73→#[async_trait]
    74→pub trait FeedbackCollector: Send + Sync {
    75→    /// Collector name.
    76→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
    77→
    78→    /// Collect feedback for a completed agent execution.
    79→    async fn collect(
    80→        &self,
    81→        execution_id: &str,
    82→        template_name: &str,
    83→        result: &ExecutionResult,
    84→    ) -> Result<Vec<FeedbackSignal>>;
    85→}
    86→
    87→pub struct FeedbackSignal {
    88→    /// What was measured (e.g., "pr_merge_rate", "build_success", "user_satisfaction").
    89→    pub metric: String,
    90→    /// The measured value.
    91→    pub value: f64,
    92→    /// When the feedback was collected.
    93→    pub timestamp: i64,
    94→    /// Links to the execution that produced this feedback.
    95→    pub execution_id: String,
    96→}
    97→```
    98→
    99→Feedback signals are stored as Engrams and used by:
   100→- **Prompt experiments** (A/B testing): Which prompt variant produces better outcomes?
   101→- **CascadeRouter**: Which model tiers produce the best cost/quality tradeoff?
   102→- **Adaptive gate thresholds**: Should gates be stricter or more lenient?
   103→
   104→### Integration
   105→
   106→The Integration trait is a higher-level abstraction that combines an EventSource with tool
   107→registration:
   108→
   109→```rust
   110→#[async_trait]
   111→pub trait Integration: Send + Sync {
   112→    /// Integration name (used as MCP server namespace).
   113→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   114→
   115→    /// Register tools provided by this integration.
   116→    fn register_tools(&self, registry: &mut ToolRegistry);
   117→
   118→    /// Event source (if this integration produces events).
   119→    fn event_source(&self) -> Option<Box<dyn EventSource>>;
   120→
   121→    /// Feedback collector (if this integration provides outcome feedback).
   122→    fn feedback_collector(&self) -> Option<Box<dyn FeedbackCollector>>;
   123→
   124→    /// Initialize the integration with configuration.
   125→    async fn init(&self, config: &serde_json::Value) -> Result<()>;
   126→
   127→    /// Shutdown the integration gracefully.
   128→    async fn shutdown(&self) -> Result<()>;
   129→}
   130→```
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→## Domain Plugin Pattern
   135→
   136→Adding a new domain to Roko follows the 8-step pattern from
   137→`refactoring-prd/10-developer-guide.md`:
   138→
   139→### Step 1: Create a Domain Crate
   140→
   141→```bash
   142→cargo new --lib crates/roko-domain-medical
   143→```
   144→
   145→```toml
   146→# crates/roko-domain-medical/Cargo.toml
   147→[dependencies]
   148→roko-core = { path = "../roko-core" }
   149→roko-plugin = { path = "../roko-plugin" }
   150→async-trait = "0.1"
   151→serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
   152→serde_json = "1"
   153→```
   154→
   155→### Step 2: Define Custom Kinds
   156→
   157→```rust
   158→use roko_core::kind::Kind;
   159→
   160→// Use reverse-DNS prefix for custom kinds
   161→pub const PATIENT_RECORD: Kind = Kind::Custom("medical.patient_record");
   162→pub const DIAGNOSIS: Kind = Kind::Custom("medical.diagnosis");
   163→pub const TREATMENT_PLAN: Kind = Kind::Custom("medical.treatment_plan");
   164→pub const LAB_RESULT: Kind = Kind::Custom("medical.lab_result");
   165→```
   166→
   167→### Step 3: Implement Domain Gates
   168→
   169→```rust
   170→use roko_core::gate::{Gate, Verdict};
   171→
   172→pub struct HipaaComplianceGate;
   173→
   174→#[async_trait]
   175→impl Gate for HipaaComplianceGate {
   176→    async fn verify(&self, engram: &Engram) -> Result<Verdict> {
   177→        // Check that output doesn't contain PII
   178→        // Verify data handling complies with HIPAA
   179→        // Return Pass/Fail with explanation
   180→    }
   181→}
   182→
   183→pub struct MedicalAccuracyGate {
   184→    /// Reference database for fact-checking.
   185→    reference_db: Arc<MedicalReferenceDB>,
   186→}
   187→
   188→#[async_trait]
   189→impl Gate for MedicalAccuracyGate {
   190→    async fn verify(&self, engram: &Engram) -> Result<Verdict> {
   191→        // Cross-reference claims against medical literature
   192→        // Flag unsupported diagnoses or treatments
   193→    }
   194→}
   195→```
   196→
   197→### Step 4: Implement Domain Scorer
   198→
   199→```rust
   200→use roko_core::scorer::Scorer;
   201→
   202→pub struct MedicalRelevanceScorer;
   203→
   204→impl Scorer for MedicalRelevanceScorer {
   205→    fn score(&self, engram: &Engram) -> Score {
   206→        // Score based on medical relevance
   207→        // Factor in patient context, condition severity, evidence quality
   208→    }
   209→}
   210→```
   211→
   212→### Step 5: Define 8D Somatic Strategy Space
   213→
   214→```rust
   215→pub struct MedicalSomaticSpace {
   216→    // What dimensions matter for medical decision-making?
   217→    pub diagnostic_confidence: f64,    // How certain is the diagnosis?
   218→    pub treatment_urgency: f64,        // How urgent is intervention?
   219→    pub patient_complexity: f64,       // How many comorbidities?
   220→    pub evidence_strength: f64,        // How strong is the evidence?
   221→    pub risk_tolerance: f64,           // Patient/provider risk preference
   222→    pub resource_availability: f64,    // Available treatments/specialists
   223→    pub regulatory_constraint: f64,    // HIPAA, insurance requirements
   224→    pub patient_preference: f64,       // Patient's stated preferences
   225→}
   226→```
   227→
   228→### Step 6: Add T0 Probes
   229→
   230→```rust
   231→pub fn medical_t0_probes() -> Vec<T0Probe> {
   232→    vec![
   233→        T0Probe::new("vitals_check", || {
   234→            // Zero-LLM check: are patient vitals in normal range?
   235→        }),
   236→        T0Probe::new("allergy_check", || {
   237→            // Zero-LLM check: does proposed treatment conflict with known allergies?
   238→        }),
   239→        T0Probe::new("interaction_check", || {
   240→            // Zero-LLM check: drug interaction screening
   241→        }),
   242→    ]
   243→}
   244→```
   245→
   246→### Step 7: Configure in roko.toml
   247→
   248→```toml
   249→[agent]
   250→domain = "medical"
   251→model = "claude-opus-4-6"
   252→temperament = "conservative"  # medical domain defaults to conservative
   253→
   254→[gates]
   255→pipeline = ["hipaa_compliance", "medical_accuracy", "llm_judge"]
   256→
   257→[substrate]
   258→type = "encrypted_file"  # HIPAA requires encryption at rest
   259→```
   260→
   261→### Step 8: Register at Init
   262→
   263→```rust
   264→pub fn register_domain(engine: &mut Engine) {
   265→    // Register custom kinds
   266→    engine.register_kind(PATIENT_RECORD);
   267→    engine.register_kind(DIAGNOSIS);
   268→
   269→    // Register gates
   270→    engine.register_gate(Box::new(HipaaComplianceGate));
   271→    engine.register_gate(Box::new(MedicalAccuracyGate::new(reference_db)));
   272→
   273→    // Register scorer
   274→    engine.register_scorer(Box::new(MedicalRelevanceScorer));
   275→
   276→    // Register T0 probes
   277→    for probe in medical_t0_probes() {
   278→        engine.register_probe(probe);
   279→    }
   280→}
   281→```
   282→
   283→**No core changes required.** The cognitive loop, Neuro knowledge tiers, Daimon affect, Dreams
   284→consolidation, and C-Factor tracking all work automatically with the domain's trait
   285→implementations.
   286→
   287→---
   288→
   289→## Plugin Loading Mechanisms
   290→
   291→Three mechanisms for loading plugins, described in
   292→`refactoring-prd/10-developer-guide.md` §6.4:
   293→
   294→### 1. Cargo Workspace Members (Compile-Time)
   295→
   296→Domain plugins implemented as workspace crates are compiled into the binary:
   297→
   298→```toml
   299→# Cargo.toml (workspace)
   300→[workspace]
   301→members = [
   302→    "crates/roko-core",
   303→    "crates/roko-std",
   304→    "crates/roko-domain-chain",    # Chain domain plugin
   305→    "crates/roko-domain-medical",  # Medical domain plugin
   306→]
   307→```
   308→
   309→**Advantages:** Full type safety, no runtime overhead, IDE support.
   310→**Disadvantages:** Requires recompilation for changes.
   311→
   312→### 2. Config-Declared (Runtime)
   313→
   314→Plugins declared in `roko.toml` are loaded at runtime:
   315→
   316→```toml
   317→[[plugins]]
   318→name = "medical"
   319→path = "./plugins/roko-domain-medical.so"  # Dynamic library
   320→config = { reference_db = "/data/medical-refs.db" }
   321→```
   322→
   323→**Advantages:** No recompilation, hot-reloadable.
   324→**Disadvantages:** Dynamic linking complexity, platform-specific.
   325→
   326→### 3. MCP Tool Discovery (Runtime)
   327→
   328→MCP servers are discovered and loaded at runtime via the MCP protocol:
   329→
   330→```toml
   331→[[agent.mcp_servers]]
   332→name = "medical-tools"
   333→command = "roko-mcp-medical"
   334→env = { MEDICAL_DB = "/data/medical.db" }
   335→```
   336→
   337→**Advantages:** Language-agnostic, process isolation, easy deployment.
   338→**Disadvantages:** IPC overhead (~1-5ms per call), no compile-time type checking.
   339→
   340→---
   341→
   342→## Plugin Lifecycle
   343→
   344→```
   345→Discovery → Validation → Initialization → Running → Shutdown
   346→    |            |             |              |          |
   347→    v            v             v              v          v
   348→  Find       Validate      Call init()    Handle     Call shutdown()
   349→  plugins    config/deps   with config    events     gracefully
   350→```
   351→
   352→### Validation
   353→
   354→On load, plugins are validated:
   355→- Config schema matches expected format
   356→- Required dependencies are available
   357→- Version compatibility with roko-core
   358→- No conflicting tool names with existing plugins
   359→
   360→### Health Monitoring
   361→
   362→The runtime monitors plugin health:
   363→- EventSource heartbeat (is it still producing events?)
   364→- Error rate tracking (too many failures -> circuit breaker)
   365→- Resource usage monitoring (memory, file descriptors)
   366→- Automatic restart on crash (with exponential backoff)
   367→
   368→---
   369→
   370→## EventSource: built-in implementation patterns
   371→
   372→Three built-in `EventSource` implementations ship with `roko-plugin`:
   373→
   374→### Cron scheduler
   375→
   376→```rust
   377→/// Cron-based event source. Emits events on a schedule.
   378→pub struct CronEventSource {
   379→    /// Cron expression (e.g., "0 */5 * * * *" for every 5 minutes).
   380→    schedule: cron::Schedule,
   381→    /// Event kind to emit (e.g., "scheduler.cron.5min").
   382→    event_kind: String,
   383→    /// Payload template (static JSON injected into every event).
   384→    payload: serde_json::Value,
   385→    /// Cancellation token.
   386→    cancel: tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken,
   387→}
   388→
   389→#[async_trait]
   390→impl EventSource for CronEventSource {
   391→    fn name(&self) -> &str { "cron" }
   392→
   393→    async fn start(&self, sender: EventSender) -> Result<()> {
   394→        loop {
   395→            let next = self.schedule.upcoming(chrono::Utc).next()
   396→                .ok_or_else(|| anyhow::anyhow!("No upcoming schedule"))?;
   397→            let delay = (next - chrono::Utc::now()).to_std()?;
   398→
   399→            tokio::select! {
   400→                _ = tokio::time::sleep(delay) => {
   401→                    sender.send(SourceEvent {
   402→                        kind: self.event_kind.clone(),
   403→                        body: self.payload.clone(),
   404→                        source: "cron".into(),
   405→                    }).await?;
   406→                }
   407→                _ = self.cancel.cancelled() => break,
   408→            }
   409→        }
   410→        Ok(())
   411→    }
   412→
   413→    async fn stop(&self) -> Result<()> {
   414→        self.cancel.cancel();
   415→        Ok(())
   416→    }
   417→
   418→    fn is_healthy(&self) -> bool {
   419→        !self.cancel.is_cancelled()
   420→    }
   421→}
   422→```
   423→
   424→### Filesystem watcher
   425→
   426→```rust
   427→/// Watches directories for file changes, emits events on modification.
   428→pub struct FsWatcherEventSource {
   429→    watch_paths: Vec<PathBuf>,
   430→    /// Debounce duration to coalesce rapid changes.
   431→    debounce: Duration,
   432→    cancel: tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken,
   433→}
   434→
   435→#[async_trait]
   436→impl EventSource for FsWatcherEventSource {
   437→    fn name(&self) -> &str { "fs_watcher" }
   438→
   439→    async fn start(&self, sender: EventSender) -> Result<()> {
   440→        let (tx, mut rx) = tokio::sync::mpsc::channel(100);
   441→        let mut watcher = notify::recommended_watcher(move |event| {
   442→            let _ = tx.blocking_send(event);
   443→        })?;
   444→
   445→        for path in &self.watch_paths {
   446→            watcher.watch(path, notify::RecursiveMode::Recursive)?;
   447→        }
   448→
   449→        // Debounce loop.
   450→        let mut pending: HashMap<PathBuf, Instant> = HashMap::new();
   451→        loop {
   452→            tokio::select! {
   453→                Some(event) = rx.recv() => {
   454→                    if let Ok(event) = event {
   455→                        for path in event.paths {
   456→                            pending.insert(path, Instant::now());
   457→                        }
   458→                    }
   459→                }
   460→                _ = tokio::time::sleep(self.debounce) => {
   461→                    let now = Instant::now();
   462→                    let ready: Vec<PathBuf> = pending.iter()
   463→                        .filter(|(_, t)| now.duration_since(**t) >= self.debounce)
   464→                        .map(|(p, _)| p.clone())
   465→                        .collect();
   466→                    for path in ready {
   467→                        pending.remove(&path);
   468→                        sender.send(SourceEvent {
   469→                            kind: "watcher.fs_change".into(),
   470→                            body: serde_json::json!({ "path": path.display().to_string() }),
   471→                            source: "fs_watcher".into(),
   472→                        }).await?;
   473→                    }
   474→                }
   475→                _ = self.cancel.cancelled() => break,
   476→            }
   477→        }
   478→        Ok(())
   479→    }
   480→
   481→    async fn stop(&self) -> Result<()> {
   482→        self.cancel.cancel();
   483→        Ok(())
   484→    }
   485→
   486→    fn is_healthy(&self) -> bool { !self.cancel.is_cancelled() }
   487→}
   488→```
   489→
   490→### Webhook receiver
   491→
   492→```rust
   493→/// HTTP webhook receiver. Listens for incoming POST requests
   494→/// and converts them to SourceEvents.
   495→pub struct WebhookEventSource {
   496→    bind_addr: std::net::SocketAddr,
   497→    /// HMAC secret for verifying webhook signatures (GitHub, Slack).
   498→    hmac_secret: Option<String>,
   499→    cancel: tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken,
   500→}

   505→## FeedbackCollector: full definition
   506→
   507→```rust
   508→use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
   509→
   510→/// Result of an agent execution, passed to feedback collectors.
   511→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   512→pub struct ExecutionResult {
   513→    /// Whether the execution succeeded.
   514→    pub success: bool,
   515→    /// Gate verdicts from the verification pipeline.
   516→    pub gate_verdicts: Vec<GateVerdict>,
   517→    /// Total tokens consumed.
   518→    pub tokens_used: u64,
   519→    /// Wall-clock duration.
   520→    pub duration_ms: u64,
   521→    /// Output artifacts (file paths, PR URLs, etc.).
   522→    pub artifacts: Vec<String>,
   523→}
   524→
   525→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   526→pub struct GateVerdict {
   527→    pub gate_name: String,
   528→    pub passed: bool,
   529→    pub confidence: f64,
   530→    pub message: String,
   531→}
   532→
   533→#[async_trait]
   534→pub trait FeedbackCollector: Send + Sync {
   535→    /// Collector name.
   536→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   537→
   538→    /// Collect feedback for a completed agent execution.
   539→    /// Returns zero or more feedback signals. Zero signals means
   540→    /// the collector has no feedback for this execution type.
   541→    async fn collect(
   542→        &self,
   543→        execution_id: &str,
   544→        template_name: &str,
   545→        result: &ExecutionResult,
   546→    ) -> Result<Vec<FeedbackSignal>>;
   547→
   548→    /// Whether this collector applies to a given template.
   549→    /// Default: applies to all templates.
   550→    fn applies_to(&self, template_name: &str) -> bool {
   551→        let _ = template_name;
   552→        true
   553→    }
   554→}
   555→
   556→/// A measured feedback signal. Stored as Engrams and consumed by
   557→/// the learning subsystem (prompt experiments, cascade router, adaptive gates).
   558→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
   559→pub struct FeedbackSignal {
   560→    /// What was measured (e.g., "pr_merge_rate", "build_success").
   561→    pub metric: String,
   562→    /// The measured value. Interpretation depends on metric:
   563→    /// - Boolean metrics: 0.0 = false, 1.0 = true
   564→    /// - Rate metrics: 0.0..1.0
   565→    /// - Duration metrics: milliseconds
   566→    /// - Cost metrics: USD
   567→    pub value: f64,
   568→    /// When the feedback was collected (Unix ms).
   569→    pub timestamp: i64,
   570→    /// Links to the execution that produced this feedback.
   571→    pub execution_id: String,
   572→    /// Optional metadata (e.g., PR number, test name).
   573→    pub metadata: serde_json::Value,
   574→}
   575→```

   578→
   579→## Integration: full definition
   580→
   581→```rust
   582→#[async_trait]
   583→pub trait Integration: Send + Sync {
   584→    /// Integration name (used as MCP server namespace).
   585→    fn name(&self) -> &str;
   586→
   587→    /// Semantic version of this integration.
   588→    fn version(&self) -> &str;
   589→
   590→    /// Register tools provided by this integration.
   591→    /// Tools are namespaced: "{integration_name}.{tool_name}".
   592→    fn register_tools(&self, registry: &mut ToolRegistry);
   593→
   594→    /// Event source (if this integration produces events).
   595→    fn event_source(&self) -> Option<Box<dyn EventSource>>;
   596→
   597→    /// Feedback collector (if this integration provides outcome feedback).
   598→    fn feedback_collector(&self) -> Option<Box<dyn FeedbackCollector>>;
   599→
   600→    /// Initialize the integration with configuration.
   601→    /// Called once at startup. Must not block.
   602→    async fn init(&self, config: &serde_json::Value) -> Result<()>;
   603→
   604→    /// Shutdown the integration gracefully.
   605→    /// Called on runtime shutdown. Must complete within 10 seconds.
   606→    async fn shutdown(&self) -> Result<()>;
   607→
   608→    /// Report integration health.
   609→    fn health(&self) -> IntegrationHealth;
   610→
   611→    /// Configuration schema (JSON Schema) for validation.
   612→    fn config_schema(&self) -> serde_json::Value {
   613→        serde_json::json!({})
   614→    }
   615→
   616→    /// Declare dependencies on other integrations.
   617→    /// The runtime ensures dependencies are initialized first.
   618→    fn dependencies(&self) -> Vec<String> {
   619→        vec![]
   620→    }
   621→}
   622→
   623→#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
   624→pub struct IntegrationHealth {
   625→    pub status: HealthStatus,
   626→    pub last_event_at: Option<i64>,
   627→    pub error_count: u64,
   628→    pub uptime_ms: u64,
   629→}
   630→
   631→#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq)]
   632→pub enum HealthStatus {
   633→    Healthy,
   634→    Degraded { reason: String },
   635→    Unhealthy { reason: String },
   636→}
   637→```
